entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00023
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00023.
The root text declares LANG="enm lat" and the file contains 8 DIV1, 247 DIV2, 13,206 L, 1,931 LG, 4,870 NOTE, 439 PB, and 238 MILESTONE elements.
Excluding L elements nested in editorial NOTE, the work body contains 12,535 encoded verse lines: 287 in the proem and 12,248 in the poem division.

entities.description: A public structured digital text whose bibliographic record names Ellis's 1812 volume but whose encoded matter selects the 1812 and 1543 title leaves, Grafton's dedication and preface, Hardyng's proem, and Hardyng's second-version Chronicle through printed page 429.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00023
notes: The U-M item page applies No Copyright–United States to the digital materials.
The exact original keying date and workflow were not found; the repository XML records a 2016 structural and proofing revision.

bibliographic.citation: John Hardyng, The Chronicle of Iohn Hardyng, containing an account of public transactions from the earliest period of English history to the beginning of the reign of King Edward the Fourth, together with the continuation by Richard Grafton, ed. Henry Ellis (London: printed for F. C. and J. Rivington et al., 1812).
bibliographic.publisher: F. C. and J. Rivington; T. Payne; Wilkie and Robinson; Longman, Hurst, Rees, Orme, and Brown; Cadell and Davies; J. Mawman; R. H. Evans
bibliographic.title: The Chronicle of Iohn Hardyng

dateStatements.label: title-page publication
dateStatements.notes: Ellis dates his closing preface note 3 January 1813, but the edition's title page is 1812.

entities.description: Henry Ellis's reset and collated edition of Hardyng's second version together with Richard Grafton's prose continuation, an Ellis biographical and literary preface, and an index.
entities.label: Ellis, The Chronicle of Iohn Hardyng, 1812
notes: Ellis identifies Harley MS 661 as the principal manuscript source of his collation and thanks Philip Bliss for principal collations from Arch. Selden. B. 10.
The edition prefers the more copious of Grafton's two January 1543 prints and records the other print's variants in the margin.
Unreferenced square brackets identify words or sentences absent from Harley 661; brackets tied to marginal references display manuscript readings.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1812, long before the current U.S. public-domain threshold for published works stated in Copyright Office Circular 15A.
rights.component: 1812 printed edition, including Ellis's editorial text and apparatus

access.entityLabel: Grafton, The Chronicle of Ihon Hardyng, 1543 (STC 12766.7)

bibliographic.citation: John Hardyng, The chronicle of Ihon Hardyng in metre (London: Richard Grafton, January 1543), STC 12766.7, ESTC S103772.
bibliographic.title: The chronicle of Ihon Hardyng in metre

entities.description: The fuller of Richard Grafton's two January 1543 printings of Hardyng's second version, followed by a lengthy prose continuation through Henry VIII; Ellis chose this print's text for his 1812 edition.
entities.label: Grafton, The Chronicle of Ihon Hardyng, 1543 (STC 12766.7)
notes: The Folger vendor record notes a cancel bifolium variant and identifies its EEBO reproduction as Early English Books, 1475–1640, reel 44:1.
Peverley's survey identifies this as the first of Grafton's two prints and the one with the longer continuation.

rights.basis: The print was published in 1543, long before the current U.S. public-domain threshold for published works.
rights.component: 1543 printed text
notes: This does not waive EEBO subscription or provider terms governing access to modern page images.

access.accessMethod: Request the manuscript for supervised consultation under current British Library reader-room procedures.
Submit a digitisation-services request describing the required folios and physical-object views.
access.format: New digital photography, subject to condition and service approval
notes: A complete-object request should explicitly include both boards, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and blanks, fols. 1r–190r, map leaves, and a color target or scale where available.
Supply and permission to reproduce are separate questions under the current service terms.

rights.basis: The British Library imaging terms distinguish image supply from permission to reproduce and reserve service conditions for delivered files.
rights.component: Any newly supplied British Library digital images
notes: No such images were obtained or cached during this review.

entities.description: A richly decorated parchment witness of Hardyng's second version, including genealogical trees, a three-page map of Scotland, appended verses, and the maker's excusation.
entities.label: British Library Harley MS 661
notes: Ellis describes this as the source of his principal manuscript collations.
The current British Library record says digitized content exists but that the images are unavailable.

physicalDescription.extent: 1* + 190 folios, with six unfoliated paper flyleaves at the beginning and five at the end
notes: Hardyng's Chronicle occupies fols. 1r–186r; f. 186v is blank.
The map occupies fols. 187r–188v; appended title-to-Scotland verses occupy fols. 188v–189r; the excusation occupies fols. 189r–190r.
Post-1600 binding; the catalog does not enumerate boards, spine, pastedowns, or every flyleaf image in the currently unavailable digital surrogate.

rights.basis: The manuscript was produced c. 1475; the historical content predates copyright, independently of rights or service terms in any modern photography.
rights.component: Fifteenth-century manuscript text and decoration as historical content

rights.basis: The British Library imaging terms distinguish image supply from permission to reproduce and reserve service conditions for delivered files.
rights.component: Any newly supplied British Library digital images
notes: No such images were obtained or cached during this review.

access.accessMethod: Submit an Imaging Services order with the shelfmark and a complete-object shot list.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS Arch. Selden. B. 10
access.format: New digital photography, subject to condition and service approval
notes: The current price page lists a minimum charge and per-image fees, but the cost of a complete codex must be quoted by the service.
A complete-object request should explicitly include bindings, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and blanks, fols. 1–212, and the physical transition between the two composite parts.

rights.basis: Bodleian Imaging Services supplies new files for a fee, and reuse remains governed by item-specific rights and Digital Bodleian terms.
rights.component: Any newly ordered Bodleian digital images

entities.description: A parchment composite codex whose first part contains Hardyng's second version and whose later part contains extracts from Lydgate's Fall of Princes.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Arch. Selden. B. 10
notes: Ellis's preface says many Selden readings agree with Grafton's print and thanks Philip Bliss for the principal collations from this manuscript.
Only thirteen selected slide images were verified online on 2026-07-12; no public whole-codex digital surrogate was found.

physicalDescription.extent: Composite codex: Hardyng part fols. 1–199; later Lydgate part fols. 200–212
notes: The catalog records fine historiated diagrams, borders, initials, and maps in the Hardyng part.
A former silken or velvet cover with brass bosses and gilt leaves has disappeared.
Acquired from John Selden's collection in 1659.

rights.basis: The codex parts date to c. 1470–1480 and c. 1520; their historical content predates copyright, independently of rights in modern slide photography.
rights.component: Fifteenth- and early-sixteenth-century manuscript text and decoration as historical content

rights.basis: Bodleian Imaging Services supplies new files for a fee, and reuse remains governed by item-specific rights and Digital Bodleian terms.
rights.component: Any newly ordered Bodleian digital images

next_steps: Transcribe Ellis's complete apparatus and resolve every siglum against current shelfmarks and print-copy records.
Consult A. Hanham's textual studies and Peverley's dissertation or later second-version editorial work for a modern assessment of Ellis's source use.
notes: The manifest records Ellis's explicitly named preferred print, duplicate print, Harley 661, and Arch. Selden. B. 10 without claiming that these exhaust all consulted witnesses.
openQuestions.question: Which exact exemplars, manuscript sigla, and reading-level dependencies underlie every part of Ellis's 1812 Hardyng text?

next_steps: Ask the University of Michigan Library for legacy project documentation or file provenance for CME00023.
Compare the repository artifact with any export obtainable directly from the current U-M platform.
openQuestions.question: When and by what exact keying, proofreading, and transformation workflow was the original U-M CME digital text produced?

next_steps: Recheck the cataloged Universal Viewer after the British Library restores unavailable digitized images.
Submit a digitisation-services request with an explicit whole-object shot list and ask whether existing master images can be supplied.
notes: The public viewer was unavailable on the review date, so no manuscript image file was cached.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete, current image surrogate of Harley MS 661 be obtained, including the binding, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and blanks, fols. 1r–190r, and color or scale targets?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Imaging Services whether an existing complete master sequence is available.
If new imaging is needed, request both boards, spine, pastedowns, every flyleaf and blank, fols. 1–212, and views documenting the boundary between composite parts.
notes: The cached IIIF bundles remain explicitly partial and do not stand in for a continuous codex surrogate.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete physical-object image surrogate of MS. Arch. Selden. B. 10 be obtained beyond the thirteen selected online slides?

next_steps: Consult Peverley's detailed stemmatic and biographical studies and newer work on the Yorkist and Edwardian revision strata.
Keep revision stages separate from the later dates of surviving manuscript copies.
notes: Current evidence supports revision after the 1457 first-version presentation and continuation into or after September 1464, but not a single secure production place.
openQuestions.question: What is the most defensible narrow chronology and place or places for Hardyng's successive revisions of the unfinished second version?

next_steps: Collate all XML DIV2/@N values and printed headings against the 1543 prints and principal manuscripts.
Avoid publishing a canonical chapter count from the 247 XML containers until that collation is complete.
notes: One final Scotland itinerary is unnumbered, and the inherited headings include repetitions and a container whose XML N does not match its printed roman numeral.
openQuestions.question: How should the inherited, repeated, and inconsistent chapter numerals in the 1812 source and CME XML map to a canonical second-version chapter sequence?

reviewNotes: The cataloged intellectual unit is Hardyng's second version, not the complete 1812 printed volume and not Grafton's prose continuation.
The fuller Grafton print is an edition-level base for Ellis; Harley 661 and Arch. Selden. B. 10 are collated witnesses, not interchangeable base manuscripts.
Selected Bodleian slide images and the complete 1812 book scan are reproduction layers with different coverage and rights from the manuscripts and historical texts.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00023 and encodes John Hardyng's unfinished Yorkist, or second, version of his verse Chronicle from Henry Ellis's 1812 edition. The XML retains the 1812 title leaf and Richard Grafton's 1543 title, dedication, and preface, then encodes Hardyng's proem and Chronicle through printed page 429; it does not encode Ellis's biographical preface, Grafton's prose continuation on pages 431–607, or the index advertised by the enclosing title. Ellis preferred the fuller of Grafton's two January 1543 prints, now STC 12766.7, noted variants from STC 12767, collated chiefly against British Library Harley MS 661, and incorporated collations from Bodleian MS Arch. Selden. B. 10. A complete 713-page scan of the 1812 volume is cached. Harley 661's cataloged digitized images are currently unavailable; Arch. Selden. B. 10 has only thirteen selected slide images online, all cached as explicitly partial bundles with separate image-license claims.

catalogSummary.author: John Hardyng

catalogSummary.title: The Chronicle of John Hardyng

catalogingSubject.label: The Chronicle of John Hardyng (second version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is John Hardyng's unfinished Yorkist, or second, version of his Middle English verse Chronicle, revised after the first-version presentation of 1457 and continued for Edward IV. It includes Hardyng's proem, the verse historical narrative, and the final unnumbered Scotland itinerary represented in CME00023. It excludes Hardyng's distinct first version, Richard Grafton's prose continuation, Henry Ellis's nineteenth-century biographical preface and apparatus as independent modern authorship, the physical manuscripts and printed books, and the U-M and repository encodings as digital layers.

contentParts.label: Proem
contentParts.summary: Hardyng introduces his age, purpose, historical undertaking, authorities, and intention to provide instruction and political counsel.

contentParts.label: Chronicle in numbered XML containers
notes: There are 246 DIV2 containers with an N attribute, but inherited heading numerals include repetitions, gaps, and disagreements; item_count is therefore an encoding count, not a canonical count of medieval chapters.
contentParts.summary: The main historical sequence from Albion and Brutus through Hardyng's account of Britain's and England's rulers, genealogies, wars, and claims, ending with the maker's excusation and counsel to Edward IV.

contentParts.label: Final unnumbered Scotland itinerary
notes: Ellis's footnote says this whole section from p. 422 is omitted in Harley MS 661; the section's reading-level dependence on other sources remains an editorial question.
contentParts.summary: A route-and-distance account for moving armies by land and water through major Scottish places, ending with proposed coordinated campaigning and a renewed address to the patron.

assertion.notes: Peverley describes revision first for Richard, duke of York, then continuation for Edward IV after December 1460. The METS introduction notes that the last datable entry cannot predate September 1464. No single completion year is supplied because the work remained unfinished.

dateStatements.display: Revised after the first-version presentation of 1457 and still in progress in or after September 1464; left unfinished

assertion.notes: STC 12766.7 is the fuller first printing; STC 12767 is a shorter revised printing from the same month.

dateStatements.display: First printed by Richard Grafton at London in January 1543, in two distinct impressions or editions
Legendary origins of Britain through the opening years of Edward IV's reign and a concluding itinerary for campaigning in Scotland

extent.description: Encoding-specific intrinsic-work metrics: the BODY has one proem DIV1 and one poem DIV1 containing 247 DIV2 containers, for 248 modeled section containers. Excluding 300 L elements nested inside editorial NOTE elements leaves 12,535 direct verse lines in 1,807 LG elements: 287 lines in 41 proem groups and 12,248 lines in 1,766 poem groups. Of the poem total, 246 numbered containers hold 12,024 lines in 1,734 groups and the final unnumbered Scotland itinerary holds 224 lines in 32 groups. These counts do not establish canonical authorial lineation or chapter numbering.

assertion.notes: Prose chapter arguments, editorial notes, headings, and prefatory prose in the source edition do not make the cataloged Hardyng work a mixed prose-and-verse composition.

formStatements.description: A long Middle English historical chronicle predominantly in seven-line stanzas, represented by 12,535 direct work-text L elements in 1,807 LG elements across the proem and poem divisions.

assertion.notes: The work constructs dynastic and political history for particular fifteenth-century audiences rather than serving as a neutral record of events.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Modern English in Ellis's apparatus and Grafton's prefatory prose belongs to edition or paratext layers rather than the cataloged medieval work's primary language statement.

scope.description: Latin phrases and short passages embedded within the primarily Middle English work

notes: The catalog record describes historical claims, genealogy, territorial argument, and military planning as subjects of the work; it does not endorse the work's claims or proposed violence.
Edition, manuscript, facsimile, access, and rights facts are modeled in the linked lineage manifest rather than collapsed into work-level description.
All line and verse-group totals are reproducible encoding metrics from the pinned XML, not claims about Hardyng's autograph lineation or a complete critical text.

next_steps: Consult Peverley's detailed textual and biographical studies and subsequent second-version scholarship.
Distinguish dates of composition and revision from the later production dates of surviving manuscripts.
notes: The current record deliberately gives only England as a broad cultural origin and does not normalize an end year for an unfinished work.
openQuestions.question: What narrower chronology and place or sequence of places can be assigned to Hardyng's Yorkist and Edwardian revision stages?

next_steps: Collate XML DIV2/@N, printed roman numerals, both 1543 prints, and the principal manuscript witnesses.
Retain encoding-specific section counts until repeated, missing, and mismatched labels are resolved.
openQuestions.question: How do the CME containers and inherited 1812 headings map to a defensible canonical chapter sequence for the second version?

next_steps: Consult linguistic studies of the second-version witnesses and distinguish authorial language from scribal and print layers.
Do not infer a work-level dialect from the copied language of Harley 661, Arch. Selden. B. 10, Grafton's prints, or Ellis's reset.
openQuestions.question: Which dialect characterization, if any, can responsibly be assigned to Hardyng's second-version work independently of particular manuscripts and printed normalization?

assertion.notes: The work participates in English chronicle and Yorkist political culture; the exact location or sequence of locations in which Hardyng revised it remains unresolved.
The second version developed through more than one political and textual stage; a single completion event is not asserted.

scope.description: The distinct second-version revision and its Edwardian continuation

notes: Grafton's separate prose continuation is excluded from the work unit even though it follows Hardyng in both 1543 prints and Ellis's volume.

scope.description: The 1543 printed source layer through which Hardyng's second version reached Ellis; not medieval authorship
The reset 1812 representation used by CME00023, not the composition of the medieval work
The 1812 source text and apparatus, using two 1543 prints and manuscript readings
Principal readings supplied to Ellis from Bodleian MS Arch. Selden. B. 10

subjects.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy

assertion.notes: The statement describes the work's political and military subject matter; it does not endorse its territorial claims or proposed violence.

subjects.label: Anglo-Scottish relations, claims, geography, and military planning

summaries.value: A long Middle English verse history that begins with Albion and Brutus, traces British and English rulers through legendary, Roman, Anglo-Saxon, Danish, Norman, Plantagenet, and Lancastrian pasts, and continues into the opening of Edward IV's reign. Hardyng reshaped the Chronicle's genealogies, political counsel, and claims about England and Scotland for Yorkist and then Edwardian patrons. The unfinished second version closes in the represented text with an itinerary and program for moving armies through Scotland.

The intrinsic work text represented in CME00023 comprises a 287-line proem, 246 numbered XML chapter containers totaling 12,024 verse lines, and a final unnumbered 224-line Scotland itinerary. The inherited printed and XML chapter numerals repeat, skip, or disagree in places, so the 247 DIV2 containers are reported as encoding structures rather than promoted to a canonical chapter count.

CME00023 also preserves Ellis's 1812 title leaf and Grafton's 1543 title, dedication, and preface, but it omits Ellis's xxi-page preface, Grafton's prose continuation on printed pages 431–607, and the index. Those source-edition layers are documented in lineage and do not expand the cataloged intellectual unit beyond Hardyng's second-version verse work.

assertion.notes: The cataloging-subject label adds '(second version)' to distinguish the intellectual unit; that qualifier is not treated as part of the conventional work title.
Ellis's volume title also advertises Grafton's continuation; the title is retained for discovery without expanding the cataloged work boundary to that continuation.

work.summary: A long Middle English verse history that begins with Albion and Brutus, traces British and English rulers through legendary, Roman, Anglo-Saxon, Danish, Norman, Plantagenet, and Lancastrian pasts, and continues into the opening of Edward IV's reign. Hardyng reshaped the Chronicle's genealogies, political counsel, and claims about England and Scotland for Yorkist and then Edwardian patrons. The unfinished second version closes in the represented text with an itinerary and program for moving armies through Scotland.
